127	### Citation discipline (the hard rule)
128	
129	**Every declaration block that derives from external reference material requires three citation elements:**
130	
131	1. **`% SOURCE:` LaTeX comment** — pointer + local file. Format: `% SOURCE: <citation pointer> (read from references/<file>.md)`. The pointer is the source identifier + section/theorem/definition number + page when available (e.g. `[Hartshorne], III.5.1, p. 174` or `[Stacks Project], Tag 01V4`). The `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical names the local file you opened to produce the verbatim quote.
132	2. **`% SOURCE QUOTE:` LaTeX comment** — the **verbatim text** of the cited statement, copied character-by-character from the local reference file you just named in the `% SOURCE:` line. Verbatim means:
133	   - **Original language.** French for Bourbaki / EGA, German for Grothendieck's early notes, English for Hartshorne / Vakil / Stacks, etc. Do NOT translate.
134	   - **Original notation preserved.** If the source writes $\mathcal{O}_X^*$ and the project uses $\mathcal{O}_X^\times$, the verbatim quote keeps $\mathcal{O}_X^*$. The project-notation restatement happens in the prose body that follows, NOT in the quote.
135	   - **Every word, every symbol.** No paraphrase, no "for brevity" abbreviation. The quote is the anti-hallucination signal — a writer who actually opened the source can paste; a writer reconstructing from memory cannot reproduce the source's exact words.
136	3. **Visible `\textit{Source: <pointer>.}` line** — first line of the prose body. Renders in the PDF so a mathematician reading the typeset blueprint sees the citation at a glance.
137	
138	**For proof blocks**: include a `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` LaTeX comment **immediately before** the `\begin{proof}` environment (NOT inside it). 1	\chapter{Flat base change for the pushforward of a quasi-coherent sheaf ($i=0$)}
2	\label{chap:Cohomology_FlatBaseChange}
3	% SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Section "Cohomology and
4	% base change, I", and Tag 02KH (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex and
5	% references/stacks-coherent.md).
6	
7	\section{Setup and motivation}
8	
9	This chapter establishes the $i=0$ (i.e.\ \(H^0\) / direct-image) case of flat
10	base change: the formation of the pushforward \(f_*\mathcal{F}\) of a
11	quasi-coherent sheaf commutes with flat base change on the target. It is the
12	foundational, fully self-contained slice of the cohomology-and-base-change
13	package that ultimately underlies relative representability statements; the
14	higher derived cases \(R^i f_*\) for \(i \geq 1\) are treated separately and are
15	out of scope here.
16	
17	Throughout, \(f : X \to S\) is a morphism of schemes that is quasi-compact and
18	quasi-separated, and \(\mathcal{F}\) is a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module.
19	A base change of \(f\) along an arbitrary morphism \(g : S' \to S\) is recorded
20	by the cartesian square obtained from the fibre product
21	\(X' = X \times_S S'\):
22	\[
23	  \begin{array}{ccc}
24	    X' & \xrightarrow{\;g'\;} & X \\[2pt]
25	    {\scriptstyle f'}\big\downarrow & & \big\downarrow{\scriptstyle f} \\[2pt]
26	    S' & \xrightarrow{\;g\;} & S
27	  \end{array}
28	\]
29	Here \(f' : X' \to S'\) is the base change of \(f\), and \(g' : X' \to X\) is the
30	first projection. We write \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}\) for the pullback
31	of \(\mathcal{F}\) to \(X'\).
32	
33	\section{The canonical base-change map}
34	
35	\begin{definition}\leanok
36	[Base-change map for the pushforward]
37	  \label{def:pushforward_base_change_map}
38	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushforwardBaseChangeMap}
39	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Section "Cohomology and
40	  % base change, I", base-change diagram (\ref{equation-base-change-diagram})
41	  % (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L877--904).
42	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $f : X \to S$ be a morphism of schemes.
43	  % Let $\mathcal{F}$ be a quasi-coherent sheaf on $X$.
44	  % Suppose further that $g : S' \to S$ is any morphism of schemes. Denote
45	  % $X' = X_{S'} = S' \times_S X$ the base change of $X$ and denote
46	  % $f' : X' \to S'$ the base change of $f$.
47	  % Also write $g' : X' \to X$ the projection,
48	  % and set $\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}$."
49	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, \S\,Cohomology and base
50	  change, I.}
51	  Let \(f : X \to S\) be a morphism of schemes, \(\mathcal{F}\) a quasi-coherent
52	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module, and \(g : S' \to S\) an arbitrary morphism, giving the
53	  cartesian square above with projection \(g' : X' \to X\) and
54	  \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}\). The \emph{base-change map for the
55	  pushforward} is the canonical morphism of \(\mathcal{O}_{S'}\)-modules
56	  \[
57	    g^*\bigl(f_*\mathcal{F}\bigr) \longrightarrow f'_*\bigl((g')^*\mathcal{F}\bigr)
58	    = f'_*\mathcal{F}' .
59	  \]
60	  It is adjoint to the morphism
61	  \(f_*\mathcal{F} \to g_*\, f'_*\mathcal{F}' = f_*\,(g')_*\mathcal{F}'\) obtained
62	  by applying \(f_*\) to the unit
63	  \(\mathcal{F} \to (g')_*(g')^*\mathcal{F}\) of the
64	  \(((g')^*, (g')_*)\)-adjunction, using the commutativity
65	  \(g \circ f' = f \circ g'\) of the square.
66	\end{definition}
67	
68	\section{The affine case}
69	
70	\subsection{Locality of isomorphisms for \texttt{Scheme.Modules} morphisms}
71	
72	Before treating the affine computation we record three project-local supplements
73	to Mathlib. Mathlib provides the per-open criterion
74	(\(\mathrm{IsIso}\,\varphi \iff \forall U,\ \mathrm{IsIso}(\varphi_U)\)) and a
75	stalkwise isomorphism criterion for \(\mathrm{TopCat.Sheaf}\)-valued morphisms, but
76	it does not package the stalk-local or basis-local criterion at the level of
77	morphisms of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. The following lemmas bridge that gap; they
78	are exactly the locality tools used in the first reduction of the affine
79	base-change lemma below, where one checks the base-change map after restricting to
80	the affine opens of the base. As bespoke infrastructure they carry no external
81	citation and stand on their proof sketches.
82	
83	\begin{lemma}\leanok
84	[Stalk-local criterion for isomorphisms of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules]
85	  \label{lem:modules_isIso_iff_stalk}
86	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Modules.isIso_iff_isIso_stalkFunctor_map}
87	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(\varphi : M \to N\) a morphism of sheaves of
88	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. Then \(\varphi\) is an isomorphism if and only if the
89	  underlying morphism of abelian-group presheaves induces an isomorphism on the
90	  stalk at every point \(x \in X\).
91	\end{lemma}
92	
93	\begin{proof}
94	
95	  If \(\varphi\) is an isomorphism, then so is its image under the forgetful
96	  functor to abelian-group presheaves, and the stalk functor at each \(x\),
97	  being a functor, carries isomorphisms to isomorphisms. Conversely, assume the
98	  stalk map is an isomorphism at every \(x\). Package the underlying abelian-group
99	  presheaves of \(M\) and \(N\) as sheaves of abelian groups, and \(\varphi\) as a
100	  morphism of these sheaves; the stalkwise-isomorphism hypothesis lets us apply the
101	  \(\mathrm{TopCat.Sheaf}\)-level stalk-local isomorphism criterion to conclude that
102	  this morphism of sheaves of abelian groups is an isomorphism. Pushing it through
103	  the forgetful functor to presheaves yields an isomorphism of the underlying
104	  abelian-group presheaf morphisms, and since the forgetful functor from
105	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules to abelian-group presheaves reflects isomorphisms,
106	  \(\varphi\) itself is an isomorphism.
107	\end{proof}
108	
109	\begin{lemma}\leanok
110	[Basis-local criterion for isomorphisms of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules]
111	  \label{lem:modules_isIso_of_isBasis}
112	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Modules.isIso_of_isIso_app_of_isBasis}
113	  \uses{lem:modules_isIso_iff_stalk}
114	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, let \(\{B_i\}_{i \in \iota}\) be a family of opens whose
115	  range is a basis of the topology of \(X\), and let \(\varphi : M \to N\) be a
116	  morphism of sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. If \(\varphi\) restricts to an
117	  isomorphism on the sections over every basic open \(B_i\), then \(\varphi\) is an
118	  isomorphism.
119	\end{lemma}
120	
121	\begin{proof}
122	  \uses{lem:modules_isIso_iff_stalk}
123	  By Lemma~\ref{lem:modules_isIso_iff_stalk} it suffices to prove that the induced
124	  stalk map is bijective at each point \(x \in X\). For injectivity, a germ that
125	  vanishes at \(x\) already vanishes on some open neighbourhood, which may be
126	  shrunk to a basic open \(B_i\); on \(B_i\) the section map \(\varphi_{B_i}\) is
127	  injective, so the original section is zero, giving injectivity of the stalk map
128	  from injectivity over the basis. For surjectivity, an arbitrary germ at \(x\) is
129	  represented by a section of \(N\) over some basic open \(B_i\) containing \(x\);
130	  since \(\varphi_{B_i}\) is surjective, this section lifts to a section of \(M\)
131	  over \(B_i\), whose germ at \(x\) maps to the given germ. Thus the stalk map is
132	  bijective, and \(\varphi\) is an isomorphism.
133	\end{proof}
134	
135	\begin{lemma}\leanok
136	[Affine-open locality criterion for isomorphisms of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules]
137	  \label{lem:modules_isIso_iff_affineOpens}
138	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Modules.isIso_iff_isIso_app_affineOpens}
139	  \uses{lem:modules_isIso_of_isBasis}
140	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(\varphi : M \to N\) a morphism of sheaves of
141	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. Then \(\varphi\) is an isomorphism if and only if it
142	  restricts to an isomorphism on the sections over every affine open of \(X\).
143	\end{lemma}
144

146	  \uses{lem:modules_isIso_of_isBasis}

148	  preserves isomorphisms. For the converse, the affine opens of a scheme form a
149	  basis of its topology, so the claim is the special case of
150	  Lemma~\ref{lem:modules_isIso_of_isBasis} in which the basis is the family of

154	\subsection{The affine computation}
155	
156	The whole result rests on the following elementary computation in the affine
157	setting, where the base-change map is visibly an isomorphism because tensoring is
158	associative. Before stating it we isolate the one ingredient not yet available as
159	a ready-made result on which the affine reduction turns: the description of the
160	pushforward of a tilde-module along a morphism of affine schemes.
161	
162	We first record three bespoke \(\Gamma\)-fragment supports that compute the global
163	sections of an affine pushforward; they carry no external citation and stand on
164	their proof sketches.

166	\begin{lemma}

170	  \label{lem:globalSectionsIso_hom_comp_specMap_appTop}

172	  Let \(\varphi : R \to R'\) be a homomorphism of commutative rings, and write
173	  \(\mathrm{gs}_R : \Gamma(\operatorname{Spec} R, \top) \cong R\) and
174	  \(\mathrm{gs}_{R'} : \Gamma(\operatorname{Spec} R', \top) \cong R'\) for the
175	  global-sections isomorphisms of the structure sheaves. Then the ring square
176	  \[
177	    \mathrm{gs}_R \;\circ\; (\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{\sharp}_{\top}
178	    \;=\; \varphi \;\circ\; \mathrm{gs}_{R'}
179	  \]
180	  commutes, where \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{\sharp}_{\top}\) is the
181	  top-open component of the structure-sheaf comparison map underlying
182	  \(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\).

188	  \(\Gamma \dashv \operatorname{Spec}\) adjunction read off at the top open: the
189	  global-sections map of \(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\) is, under the
190	  global-sections isomorphisms on each side, conjugate to \(\varphi\) itself.
191	  Transposing the naturality square of the \(\operatorname{Spec}\) unit gives
192	  exactly the asserted ring equation.

442	  be an \(R'\)-module. Then there is a canonical isomorphism of
443	  \(\operatorname{Spec}(R)\)-modules
444	  \[
445	    \bigl(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\bigr)_*\,\widetilde{M}
446	    \;\cong\;

465	
466	\begin{proof}
467	  \uses{lem:modules_isIso_of_isBasis, lem:gammaPushforwardTildeIso,
468	  lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso_conditional, lem:powers_restrictScalars,
469	  lem:gammaPushforwardIsoAt, lem:tildeRestriction_isLocalizedModule}
470	  We build the isomorphism \emph{directly on a basis of basic opens}. This is the
471	  non-circular route: checking the comparison map on basic opens yields the object
472	  isomorphism, and quasi-coherence of the pushforward is then a corollary, not a
473	  prerequisite. (One must not reverse this order: the global-sections comparison
474	  \(\Gamma\bigl((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_*\widetilde{M}\bigr) \cong
475	  \operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\) of
476	  Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardTildeIso} alone would only upgrade to an object
477	  isomorphism once the pushforward is already known to be quasi-coherent, so
478	  quasi-coherence cannot be deduced from an object isomorphism built that way.)
479	
480	  \emph{The comparison morphism.} There is a canonical morphism of
481	  \(\operatorname{Spec}(R)\)-modules
482	  \[
483	    \alpha : \widetilde{\bigl(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\bigr)}
484	    \longrightarrow \bigl(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\bigr)_*\widetilde{M},
485	  \]
486	  namely the tilde-adjoint of the global-sections identification of
487	  Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardTildeIso}. We show \(\alpha\) is an isomorphism.
488	
489	  \medskip
490	  \emph{Reduction to basic opens.} The basic opens \(D(a)\), for \(a \in R\), form a
491	  basis of the topology of \(\operatorname{Spec}(R)\). By the basis-local criterion
492	  Lemma~\ref{lem:modules_isIso_of_isBasis} it suffices to check that \(\alpha\)
493	  restricts to an isomorphism on the sections over each basic open \(D(a)\).
494	
495	  \medskip
496	  \emph{The computation over \(D(a)\).} On the source, the sections of
497	  \(\widetilde{(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M)}\) over \(D(a)\) are the
498	  localization \(\bigl(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\bigr)[1/a]\) as an
499	  \(R[1/a]\)-module. On the target, since \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{-1}D(a) =
500	  D(\varphi a)\), the sections of \(\bigl(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\bigr)_*
501	  \widetilde{M}\) over \(D(a)\) are the sections of \(\widetilde{M}\) over
502	  \(D(\varphi a)\), i.e.\ the localization \(M[1/\varphi a]\), regarded as an
503	  \(R[1/a]\)-module by restriction of scalars along the induced map
504	  \(R[1/a] \to R'[1/\varphi a]\). These two localizations agree: the element \(a\)
505	  acts on \(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\) through \(\varphi a\), so localizing
506	  \(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\) at the multiplicative set generated by \(a\) is
507	  the same module as localizing \(M\) at the multiplicative set generated by
508	  \(\varphi a\). Both are the universal localization of
509	  \(M\) inverting \(\varphi a\), so the comparison map \(\alpha\) is an isomorphism
510	  on the sections over \(D(a)\). As this holds for every basic open, \(\alpha\) is
511	  an isomorphism.
512

514	  \emph{The explicit formalization route over \(D(a)\).} The agreement of the two
515	  localizations just described is the entire content, but it must be realized
516	  through the global ring \(R\), because the \(R\)-action on the pushforward
517	  sections over \(D(a)\) is built from the global-sections module of
518	  \(N := (\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_*\widetilde M\) rather than directly from
519	  \(R[1/a]\); a naive section-level identity \(a \cdot x = \varphi(a) \cdot x\) does
520	  not present itself. The reduction therefore proceeds exactly as the conditional
521	  assembly Lemma~\ref{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso_conditional} prescribes: it
522	  suffices to supply, for each \(a \in R\), the hypothesis \(\mathrm{hloc}(a)\) that
523	  the structure-sheaf restriction \(\Gamma(N, \top) \to \Gamma(N, D(a))\) exhibits
524	  \(\Gamma(N, D(a))\) as the localization of \(\Gamma(N, \top)\) at
525	  \(\mathrm{powers}(a)\). We construct \(\mathrm{hloc}(a)\) by transporting the
526	  already-known \(R'\)-side localization, in element-free fashion, along a
527	  \(D(a)\)-level analogue of the \(\top\)-level identification
528	  Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIso}. This proceeds in three movements.

531	  \(\top\)-level Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIso}, construct an \(R\)-linear
532	  isomorphism
533	  \[

539	  reconciliations of nested restriction-of-scalars towers along the structure-sheaf
540	  section maps, glued by a canonical isomorphism from the ring equation — except that the
541	  canonical isomorphism now rests on a \emph{\(D(a)\)-level} ring equation in place of
542	  the \(\top\)-level one of
543	  Lemma~\ref{lem:globalSectionsIso_hom_comp_specMap_appTop}. The localization map

  an \(\operatorname{Spec}(R')\)-module. Consider the two presheaves of
  \(R\)-modules on \(\operatorname{Spec}(R)\), each contravariant in the open
  argument \(U \subseteq \operatorname{Spec}(R)\):
  \begin{itemize}
    \item the \emph{source presheaf}
      \(\mathcal{P} : U \mapsto \Gamma\bigl((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_* N,\, U\bigr)\),
      whose restriction maps are the structure-sheaf restriction maps of the
      pushforward \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_* N\);
    \item the \emph{target presheaf}
      \(\mathcal{Q} : U \mapsto
        \operatorname{restr}_\varphi\bigl(\Gamma(N,\, (\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{-1}U)\bigr)\),
      whose restriction maps are the structure-sheaf restriction maps of \(N\)
      transported through the preimage operation
      \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{-1}\) and then read as \(R\)-linear maps by
      restriction of scalars along \(\varphi\).
  \end{itemize}
  Then the family \(\{e_U\}_U\) of \(R\)-linear isomorphisms of
  Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIsoAt} (\(e_U\) being the isomorphism at the open
  \(U\)) is a \emph{natural} isomorphism \(\mathcal{P} \cong \mathcal{Q}\). Concretely,
  for any inclusion of opens \(U' \subseteq U\), with
  \(V = (\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{-1}U\) and
  \(V' = (\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{-1}U'\), the square
  \[
    \begin{array}{ccc}
      \Gamma\bigl((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_* N,\, U\bigr)
        & \xrightarrow{\;e_U\;}
        & \operatorname{restr}_\varphi\bigl(\Gamma(N,\, V)\bigr) \\[4pt]
      {\scriptstyle \mathrm{res}}\big\downarrow & & \big\downarrow{\scriptstyle \mathrm{res}} \\[4pt]
      \Gamma\bigl((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_* N,\, U'\bigr)
        & \xrightarrow{\;e_{U'}\;}
        & \operatorname{restr}_\varphi\bigl(\Gamma(N,\, V')\bigr)
    \end{array}
  \]
  commutes, where the vertical maps are the respective structure-sheaf restriction
  maps. Equivalently, the isomorphisms \(e_U\) assemble into a single isomorphism of
  presheaves of \(R\)-modules, e.g.\ by exhibiting the components \(e_U\) as a
  natural transformation that is objectwise an isomorphism.
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
  \uses{lem:gammaPushforwardIsoAt}
  Each \(e_U\) is, by the construction of Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIsoAt}, a
  composite whose constituents are of exactly two kinds: structure-sheaf restriction
  maps (of \(N\), respectively of \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_* N\)), and
  conjugation by a \emph{fixed} ring map — the one supplied by the
  restriction-of-scalars reconciliation and by the \(\top\)-level ring equation of
  Lemma~\ref{lem:globalSectionsIso_hom_comp_specMap_appTop}, neither of which depends
  on the open \(U\). Both kinds commute with further restriction along an inclusion
  \(U' \subseteq U\): restriction maps commute by the presheaf (functoriality) axiom
  of the two structure sheaves applied to the inclusions \(U' \subseteq U\) and
  \(V' \subseteq V\) (note \(V' = (\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{-1}U' \subseteq
  (\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{-1}U = V\) since \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{-1}\)
  is monotone), and conjugation by a fixed ring map commutes with restriction because
  the ring map is independent of the open. The naturality square is therefore the
  pasting of the three component-naturality squares of the constituents of \(e_U\):
  the (inverse-)naturality of the restriction-of-scalars composition reconciliation,
  the \(\mathrm{eqToHom}\)-naturality of the top-open ring equation, and the
  naturality of the restriction-of-scalars composition for \(\varphi\). Each of these
  squares commutes for the structural reason just given, so their paste — the outer
  square — commutes. Since each \(e_U\) is moreover an isomorphism, the family is a
  natural isomorphism of the two presheaves.
\end{proof}
